# Governance Directives

Follow these directives in every file you write or edit.

## Non-Negotiable Constraints

These are invariants. Violation is never acceptable.

- Generated rules files from `verikt guide` MUST stay within 15-25 instructions, 500-1,500 tokens per file. Critical rules at start and end. Each instruction paired with motivation. 3-5 code examples included. (ref: INV-001)
- Symlinked directories MUST be skipped when traversing project directories. NEVER treat symlinks as project-local code. Check `d.Type()&fs.ModeSymlink != 0` and return `filepath.SkipDir`. (ref: INV-002)
- Templates MUST be secure by default. NEVER ship hardcoded secrets or fallback credentials. NEVER default CORS to `*`, gRPC reflection to `true`, or TLS to disabled. Auth middleware MUST validate tokens, not just check they exist. Docker images MUST pin versions. SQL MUST use parameterized queries. (ref: INV-003)

## Architecture Decisions

- NEVER implement language providers as external plugins or gRPC services. Providers are embedded Go packages compiled into the `verikt` binary. The `LanguageProvider` interface supports future extraction — do not build that extraction now. (ref: ADR-001)
- Templates use `text/template` + `embed.FS` + `manifest.yaml`. Three-file convention: manifest, wizard, files. Standard library only, NEVER use external template engines. (ref: ADR-002)
- The workflow is Declare-Distribute-Enforce. `verikt.yaml` is the declarative desired state — commands read from it, NEVER bypass it. There is no `plan` or `apply` step — the agent is the execution layer. (ref: ADR-010, supersedes ADR-003)
- Extensibility has three layers — do not conflate them. Templates are data files (YAML + text/template). Presets are shareable YAML configs. Analyzers are compiled Go code. NEVER require Go knowledge for template or preset authoring. (ref: ADR-004)
- New packages go in `internal/`. New providers go in `providers/{lang}/`. NEVER create packages in `pkg/` unless building a public Go API. NEVER put business logic in `cmd/verikt/`. (ref: ADR-005)
- Polyglot architecture: Go orchestrator (CLI, TUI, config, templates) + Rust engine (tree-sitter parsing, import graphs, rule engine). Communication via protobuf over stdin/stdout. Rust binary embedded via `//go:embed`, extracted to cache on first run. Single binary distribution. (ref: ADR-006)
- Templates MUST check feature flags, NEVER version numbers. Feature matrix maps language versions to boolean flags. Engine resolves at scaffold time from detected version. Templates use `{{if .Features.name}}`. (ref: ADR-007)
- `verikt guide` is normative — describes architectural intent from `verikt.yaml`, NEVER scans the filesystem. `verikt check` is observational — reports what the code actually looks like. Guide references checker for unmapped directories. (ref: ADR-008)
- When adding CLI features, the success metric is agent session quality — not CLI UX. If a feature doesn't improve what the agent can do, it doesn't ship. `verikt guide` feeds agents context, `verikt new` provides the source of truth, `verikt check` validates, `verikt analyze` reads existing code. (ref: ADR-009)

## Reminder: Non-Negotiable Constraints

These constraints were listed above and are restated for emphasis.
Do not violate them under any circumstances.

- Generated rules files: 15-25 instructions, 500-1,500 tokens, critical rules at start/end, motivation + examples. (ref: INV-001)
- Symlinked directories: ALWAYS skip, NEVER treat as project-local. (ref: INV-002)
- Templates MUST be secure by default: no hardcoded secrets, no permissive defaults, no unpinned images, auth MUST validate, SQL MUST be parameterized. (ref: INV-003)
